LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT STEPHEN BROWN'S MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS, AS TO THE RESCISSION CAUSE OF ACTION, IS GRANTED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. THE COURT DENIES JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS AS TO THE INTENTIONAL MISREPRESENTATION CAUSE OF ACTION. THE COURT DENIES JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS AS TO THE CONCEALMENT CAUSE OF ACTION. THE COURT DENIES JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS, AND TREATS THE CONSPIRACY ALLEGATIONS AS GENERAL CHARGING ALLEGATIONS THAT SUPPORT PLAINTIFF'S CLAIM THAT DEFENDANT CONSPIRED TO ENGAGE IN TORTIOUS CONDUCT, E.G., CONSPIRACY TO DEFRAUD. THE COURT DENIES JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS AS TO THE INDEMNITY CLAIM. ORDER SIGNED. (SEE MINI-MINUTES AND/OR ORDER FOR COMPLETE RULING). JUDGE: ERNEST H. Notice Of Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Mar-24-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling. As to DEFENDANT STEPHEN BROWN'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings, the motion for judgment on the pleadings as to the rescission cause of action is granted without leave to amend. The amended complaint shows that defendant obtained the insurance on behalf of 1725 Fulton St. LLC. (Amended Complaint, pars. 14, 27, 28, 29). 1725 Fulton St. LLC was the party to the contract, not defendant. It is improper to bring a rescission cause of action against a non-party to the contract. The court denies judgment on the pleadings as to the intentional misrepresentation cause of action. The cause of action is alleged with sufficient particularity. It is irrelevant that the misrepresentation was made to Mason rather than plaintiff. The Restatement Second of Torts, section 533, articulates the relevant principle in this way: "The maker of a fraudulent misrepresentation is subject to liability for pecuniary loss to another who acts in justifiable reliance upon it if the misrepresentation, although not made directly to the other, is made to a third person and the maker intends or has reason to expect that its terms will be repeated or its substance communicated to the other, and that it will influence his conduct in the transaction or type of transactions involved." See also Mirkin v. Wasserman (1993) 5 Cal.4th 1082, 1095. The court denies judgment on the pleadings as to the concealment cause of action. A constructive fraud/concealment cause of action can be established without a special relationship where there is exclusive knowledge, partial suppression, or active concealment. See Witkin, Torts, CA Law, Vol. 5, sec. 798-799 (2005). In other words, defendant owed a duty to plaintiff not to actively conceal information or partially disclose information in a misleading manner. Defendant is correct that conspiracy is not a distinct cause of action. The court denies judgment on the pleadings, however, and treats the conspiracy allegations as general charging allegations that support plaintiff's claim that defendant conspired to engage in tortious conduct, e.g., conspiracy to defraud. The court denies judgment on the pleadings as to the indemnity claim. Defendant's tortious conduct caused plaintiff harm. Plaintiff defended actions that it otherwise would not have. ORDER SIGNED. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Melanie Dawn Gheno, CSR #7489 (302/EHG)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-29-2014 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. DARA M. TANG OF THE HAYES SCOTT MCLAY LLP, TEL. NO. (650) 486-2899 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. PETER C. CATALANOTTI OF MANNING & KASS, TEL. NO. 217-6990 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - THE MATTER WAS DISCUSSED. The Court modified its tentative ruling and ruled as follows: DEFENDANT STEPHEN BROWN's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT - The Demurrer is sustained in part and overruled in part. As to the cause of action for rescission, the Demurrer is sustained with ten days leave for Plaintiff to allege the existence of a contract between Plaintiff and Defendant. As to the cause of action for concealment, the Demurrer is sustained with ten days leave to amend for Plaintiff to allege facts establishing Defendant's duty to disclose. Finally, the Demurrer is overruled as to the indemnity cause of action. Defendant demurred to the cause of action for concealment, but not to the cause of action for intentional misrepresentation, and similarly demurred to the indemnity cause of action solely on the ground that the concealment claim failed to establish a duty. Defendant did not address the indemnity cause of action. With respect to the cause of action for intentional misrepresentation, overruled without prejudice. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Ann Delgado, CSR #11185
